past_medical_history: Migraine without aura, infrequent (2-3 per year), managed with ibuprofen
Cesarean section at age 32 without complications
Remote history of febrile seizure at age 2, single episode per mother's report
family_history: Father with type 2 diabetes and hypertension
No known family history of epilepsy or seizures
Maternal grandmother with dementia diagnosed in her late 70s

social_history.alcohol: Occasional wine, approximately 1-2 glasses per week
social_history.living_situation: Lives with husband and 9-year-old daughter in a suburban home
social_history.driving: Drives daily for work commute and school activities
social_history.exercise: Walks 3-4 times per week, occasional yoga

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Speech is fluent without word-finding pauses or paraphasic errors. Naming and repetition intact. Follows three-step commands without difficulty. Recall is 3/3 at registration and 2/3 at five minutes (recalled third word with category cue). Affect appears mildly anxious but appropriate. No inattention or perseveration noted.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal, round, and reactive to light bilaterally (3 mm to 2 mm). Visual fields full to confrontation. Extraocular movements full without nystagmus. Facial sensation intact bilaterally in all three divisions. Face symmetric at rest and with activation. Hearing grossly intact to finger rub bilaterally. Palate elevates symmetrically. Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius strength 5/5 bilaterally. Tongue midline without atrophy or fasciculations.
neurological_exam.motor: Bulk and tone normal throughout. Strength 5/5 in all muscle groups of the upper and lower extremities bilaterally. No pronator drift. No adventitious movements.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch, pinprick, vibration, and proprioception intact in all extremities. No extinction on double simultaneous stimulation.
neurological_exam.reflexes: 2+ and symmetric at biceps, triceps, brachioradialis, patellar, and Achilles bilaterally. Plantar responses flexor bilaterally.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose and heel-to-shin performed without dysmetria bilaterally. Rapid alternating movements intact and symmetric.
neurological_exam.gait: Gait normal with steady base. Tandem gait intact. Romberg negative.
neurological_exam.additional: No tongue lacerations observed. Patient reports occasional bitten inner cheek noted upon waking in the morning on at least two occasions in the past year, which she attributed to nighttime teeth grinding.

patient.chief_complaint: Recurrent episodes of staring and unresponsiveness, with one episode witnessed by husband involving involuntary hand movements
patient.history_present_illness:
Ms. Delgado is a 41-year-old right-handed elementary school teacher who presents to the emergency department after her husband witnessed an episode of unresponsiveness accompanied by repetitive fumbling hand movements during dinner the previous evening. He reports that the patient suddenly stopped speaking mid-sentence, stared blankly ahead, and appeared to be picking at the tablecloth with her left hand while making subtle chewing motions with her mouth. The episode lasted approximately 45 to 60 seconds. When she came to, she appeared confused and asked what had happened. She was oriented again within approximately five minutes but reported feeling fatigued and complained of a mild headache.

Upon further questioning, the patient acknowledges that she has been experiencing intermittent spells over the past eight to ten months that she has difficulty describing. She notes that sometimes she will suddenly feel disconnected from her surroundings, as if she is observing herself from the outside, along with a vague uncomfortable sensation in her abdomen that she cannot further characterize. These episodes are brief, typically lasting 20 to 40 seconds, and she is generally able to continue with what she is doing afterward, though she sometimes feels momentarily foggy. She estimates these have occurred approximately once or twice per month. She initially attributed them to her anxiety disorder and discussed them with her primary care physician, who increased her sertraline dose from 25 mg to 50 mg about four months ago without clear improvement in the episodes. She denies any convulsive activity, urinary incontinence, or tongue biting during these events, though she does mention the occasional cheek biting noted on waking. She has not experienced any clear preceding headache, visual changes, or focal weakness. There is no history of head trauma, CNS infection, or prior neuroimaging. She notes that her sleep has been somewhat disrupted due to work-related stress but denies significant sleep deprivation. There was no recent illness or medication changes other than the sertraline adjustment.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 9 Hz alpha rhythm, symmetric, reactive to eye opening
background.symmetry: Mildly disorganized over the right temporal region during drowsiness
background.sleep_features: Vertex waves and sleep spindles present; sleep architecture grossly preserved

findings.frequency: Rare, 3 noted during the 45-minute recording, occurring during drowsiness
findings.morphology: Broad-based sharp transients at F8/T4 with an after-going slow wave component; morphology is somewhat atypical — not clearly epileptiform versus benign sharply contoured activity. Duration approximately 80-100 ms.

findings.frequency: Intermittent bursts of polymorphic delta activity, 3-5 second runs, seen 4-5 times during the recording
findings.morphology: Polymorphic delta activity, 1.5-3 Hz, not clearly rhythmic

artifacts.impact: Moderate, partially obscuring temporal activity during wakefulness; interpretation limited in some epochs
Intermittent electrode pop at T4 requiring careful distinction from cerebral sharp transients

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Three minutes performed; no provocation of epileptiform activity or clinical symptoms. Mild symmetric background slowing noted.
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: Normal symmetric photic driving response. No photoparoxysmal response.

eeg.impression: Mildly abnormal EEG due to intermittent right temporal polymorphic delta slowing. Rare right anterior-to-mid temporal sharp transients of equivocal epileptiform morphology were noted during drowsiness but cannot be definitively classified as epileptiform discharges based on this recording alone. No electrographic seizures captured. Clinical correlation recommended.
eeg.limitations: Routine 45-minute recording with moderate muscle artifact over temporal chains limiting interpretation in some epochs. T4 electrode artifact noted intermittently. No clinical events captured.

findings.size: Right hippocampal volume borderline reduced compared to left; automated volumetric analysis reports right hippocampus at 12th percentile for age and sex

signal_characteristics.T1: Questionable subtle loss of internal architecture in the right hippocampal body, difficult to assess due to partial volume effects
signal_characteristics.T2: Equivocal mild hyperintensity in the right hippocampal body on coronal T2-weighted images; could represent early signal change or artifact related to susceptibility from adjacent petrous bone
signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Very subtle possible hyperintensity in the right hippocampus on coronal FLAIR, but not definitively abnormal; would benefit from comparison with dedicated epilepsy protocol at 3T

findings.size: Two punctate foci, each approximately 2-3 mm

volumetrics.left_hippocampus: 3.2 cm3 (42nd percentile for age and sex)
volumetrics.right_hippocampus: 2.7 cm3 (12th percentile for age and sex)

additional_observations: No other cortical signal abnormalities or evidence of cortical dysplasia
Ventricular system normal in size and configuration
Two non-specific T2/FLAIR hyperintense foci in right frontal white matter, likely representing nonspecific gliosis or small vessel ischemic changes, unusual for this age
mri.impression: Subtle asymmetry of hippocampal volumes with the right hippocampus at the lower end of normal (12th percentile). Equivocal T2/FLAIR signal change in the right hippocampus that does not meet definitive criteria for mesial temporal sclerosis on this 1.5T study. Two non-specific punctate white matter foci in the right frontal region, of uncertain clinical significance. Clinical correlation recommended.

ecg.interpretation: Normal sinus rhythm with normal intervals and axis. No arrhythmia or conduction abnormality identified.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 9 Hz alpha, symmetric, reactive to eye opening
background.symmetry: Intermittent right temporal slowing noted, particularly during drowsiness and light sleep
background.sleep_features: Normal sleep architecture with vertex waves, K-complexes, and sleep spindles. Interictal epileptiform discharges more prominent during NREM sleep.

findings.frequency: Occasional, averaging 5-6 per hour during wakefulness, increasing to 10-12 per hour during NREM sleep
findings.morphology: Phase reversal at F8 with field extending to T4 and occasionally F4. Duration 50-70 ms with an after-going slow wave. Morphology during sleep is more clearly epileptiform than on the routine EEG.
findings.state: Present during wakefulness and sleep, maximal during NREM sleep

findings.location: Right anterior temporal onset (F8/T4), with limited spread
findings.frequency: One clinical seizure captured during 96-hour monitoring (day 3)
findings.morphology: Seizure onset with right temporal low-voltage fast activity evolving to 5-6 Hz rhythmic theta, then to 2-3 Hz rhythmic delta over 50 seconds. Seizure confined predominantly to right temporal chain without bilateral spread.
findings.state: Occurred during quiet wakefulness while patient was reading

findings.frequency: Intermittent runs during drowsiness and wakefulness
findings.morphology: Polymorphic and at times semi-rhythmic delta activity, 1.5-3 Hz, with some runs approaching TIRDA morphology

artifacts.impact: Mild, did not obscure the captured seizure or interictal discharges

output.impression: Abnormal 96-hour continuous video-EEG monitoring. One habitual clinical seizure captured with right anterior temporal electrographic onset (F8/T4), correlating with focal impaired awareness seizure semiology (behavioral arrest, oral automatisms, left hand automatisms, post-ictal confusion). Occasional right anterior temporal interictal epileptiform discharges confirmed, more clearly epileptiform during sleep. Clinical correlation recommended.
output.limitations: Only one habitual event was captured. Additional events would strengthen lateralization confidence.

findings.location: Right hippocampus, predominantly affecting the body segment
findings.size: Right hippocampal volume 2.4 cm3 (6th percentile for age and sex) versus left 3.3 cm3 (40th percentile for age and sex)

signal_characteristics.T2_coronal: Definite hyperintense signal in the right hippocampal body on thin coronal T2-weighted images (3 mm slices perpendicular to the long axis of the hippocampus)
signal_characteristics.FLAIR_coronal: Hyperintense signal in the right hippocampal body, now clearly abnormal on optimized coronal FLAIR compared to the equivocal finding on the prior 1.5T study
signal_characteristics.T1_3D: Subtle loss of normal internal architecture of the right hippocampus with reduced digitations

findings.mass_effect: None. Mild prominence of the right temporal horn compared to the left, suggesting early ex vacuo dilation.

findings.size: Two punctate foci, each 2-3 mm, unchanged from prior study

additional_observations: The right hippocampal signal abnormality is now clearly demonstrated on the optimized 3T sequences, confirming the subtle finding suspected on the prior 1.5T study
No evidence of focal cortical dysplasia
Non-specific right frontal white matter foci unchanged and likely incidental
output.impression: This was equivocal on the prior 1.5T study but is now clearly abnormal. Non-specific right frontal white matter foci, unchanged and of uncertain significance. Findings concordant with right temporal lobe epilepsy. Clinical correlation recommended.

Neuropsychological_Testing.test: Verbal Memory (CVLT-II Long Delay Free Recall)

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: Visual Memory (BVMT-R Total Learning) T-score 36 T-score (H), Visual Memory (BVMT-R Delayed Recall) T-score 33 T-score (H).
abnormal_values_summary: Visual Memory (BVMT-R Total Learning): T-score 36 T-score (L)
Visual Memory (BVMT-R Delayed Recall): T-score 33 T-score (H)

signal_characteristics.FDG_uptake: Mildly to moderately reduced FDG uptake in the right mesial temporal structures (hippocampus and parahippocampal gyrus) compared to the left. Right lateral temporal neocortex shows borderline reduced uptake.
signal_characteristics.asymmetry_index: Right-to-left temporal lobe asymmetry index 0.85 (normal >0.90), indicating mild right temporal hypometabolism

findings.location: Remainder of brain including bilateral frontal, parietal, occipital lobes, left temporal lobe, basal ganglia, thalami, and cerebellum

additional_observations: Interictal FDG-PET acquired during continuous EEG-confirmed interictal state
output.impression: Interictal FDG-PET demonstrates mild right mesial temporal hypometabolism, concordant with the right hippocampal sclerosis seen on 3T MRI and right temporal epileptiform activity on prolonged EEG. The multi-modal concordance supports right temporal lobe epilepsy. Clinical correlation recommended.

Antiepileptic_Drug_Levels.value: Not applicable — patient not on AEDs at initial presentation

output.query: temporal lobe epilepsy misdiagnosed as anxiety disorder panic attacks

results.title: Misdiagnosis of epileptic seizures as panic attacks in patients with temporal lobe epilepsy
results.source: Vazquez B, Devinsky O. Epilepsy & Behavior 2003;4(6):715-719
results.summary: Temporal lobe seizures with prominent autonomic and emotional features (fear, epigastric sensation, depersonalization) are frequently misdiagnosed as panic attacks. Average delay to correct diagnosis ranges from 1 to 7 years.

results.title: The role of prolonged video-EEG monitoring in diagnosis of paroxysmal events
results.summary: Approximately 20-30% of patients referred to epilepsy monitoring units for refractory seizures are found to have non-epileptic events. Conversely, some patients with anxiety or panic disorder diagnoses are found to have temporal lobe epilepsy on prolonged monitoring.

results.title: Subtle MRI-negative temporal lobe epilepsy: importance of 3T imaging
results.summary: 3T MRI with epilepsy protocol detects subtle hippocampal sclerosis missed on 1.5T in approximately 15-20% of cases initially reported as MRI-negative.

output.summary: Temporal lobe epilepsy is commonly misdiagnosed as anxiety or panic disorder, particularly when seizures present with prominent emotional and autonomic symptoms without convulsive activity. Diagnostic delay averages 2-5 years. Prolonged video-EEG monitoring is essential for definitive diagnosis when routine EEG is equivocal. High-field (3T) MRI with dedicated epilepsy protocol significantly improves detection of subtle hippocampal sclerosis compared to standard 1.5T imaging.

output.proposed: Levetiracetam 500 mg twice daily (titrate to 1000 mg twice daily)
interactions: Levetiracetam + Sertraline: No significant pharmacokinetic interaction. However, both medications can cause psychiatric side effects (irritability, mood changes with levetiracetam; serotonergic effects with sertraline). Monitor for cumulative neuropsychiatric adverse effects.
warnings: Both levetiracetam and sertraline may contribute to mood instability — monitor for irritability, depression, or behavioral changes
Levetiracetam-associated psychiatric adverse effects occur in 10-15% of patients and may be more common in patients with pre-existing anxiety disorders
Consider lamotrigine as an alternative if psychiatric side effects emerge, given its potential mood-stabilizing properties
output.formulary_status: On formulary, no prior authorization required
alternatives: Lamotrigine 25 mg daily, titrate to 200 mg daily — may have mood-stabilizing benefits; slower titration required
Oxcarbazepine 300 mg twice daily, titrate to 600 mg twice daily — risk of hyponatremia, monitor sodium
Lacosamide 50 mg twice daily, titrate to 200 mg twice daily — well tolerated, favorable psychiatric profile

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Focal epilepsy - temporal lobe (right mesial temporal lobe epilepsy with subtle hippocampal sclerosis)

differential.key_features: Episodes initially attributed to anxiety. However, stereotyped semiology with oral and manual automatisms, post-ictal confusion, and occasional cheek biting favor epileptic etiology. Video-EEG capturing an event with electrographic correlate definitively excludes PNES.

Patient has a pre-existing anxiety diagnosis and describes depersonalization during events. However, the brief stereotyped episodes with unresponsiveness, automatisms, and post-ictal confusion are more consistent with focal impaired awareness seizures than dissociative episodes.
No prodrome of lightheadedness or presyncope. Prolonged post-event confusion and automatisms during the events are inconsistent with typical vasovagal syncope. Normal ECG.

Patient has a history of migraine without aura, but the current episodes differ markedly — they are brief (seconds to one minute), lack the gradual spread of migrainous aura, and feature altered awareness with automatisms rather than visual or sensory symptoms.

optimal_actions.action: Obtain detailed history of the episodes including semiology, duration, frequency, associated symptoms, and witness accounts. Specifically ask about aura, automatisms, post-event confusion, and tongue or cheek biting.
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Recurrent stereotyped episodes of altered awareness with automatisms, vague aura with depersonalization and abdominal discomfort, post-ictal confusion — consistent with focal impaired awareness seizures
Normal interictal exam; subtle finding of 2/3 recall at 5 minutes may hint at temporal lobe dysfunction but is nonspecific
Equivocal right temporal sharp transients and intermittent temporal slowing — suggestive but not diagnostic

optimal_actions.action: Order MRI brain to evaluate for structural etiology
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Subtle hippocampal asymmetry, equivocal signal change on 1.5T — needs dedicated epilepsy protocol at 3T

optimal_actions.action: Obtain basic labs including BMP, CBC, TSH, and urine drug screen to exclude metabolic and toxic causes
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal metabolic panel, normal CBC, normal TSH, negative drug screen

optimal_actions.action: Request prolonged video-EEG monitoring to capture a habitual event and clarify equivocal routine EEG findings
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Capture of focal impaired awareness seizure with right temporal electrographic onset, confirming epilepsy diagnosis

optimal_actions.action: Request 3T MRI with dedicated epilepsy protocol given equivocal findings on standard MRI
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Confirmation of right mesial temporal sclerosis with definite hippocampal volume loss and signal abnormality

optimal_actions.action: Initiate antiepileptic drug therapy appropriate for focal epilepsy, checking drug interactions with sertraline
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Levetiracetam or lamotrigine as first-line options; no major pharmacokinetic interactions with sertraline but monitor for psychiatric side effects

optimal_actions.action: Counsel patient on driving restrictions, seizure safety precautions, and correct the prior misattribution of events to anxiety
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Patient informed of seizure diagnosis, driving laws, and safety measures

optimal_actions.action: Refer to comprehensive epilepsy center for ongoing management and consideration of surgical evaluation if seizures are refractory to medical therapy

critical_actions: Recognize that stereotyped episodes of altered awareness with automatisms and post-ictal confusion are more consistent with focal impaired awareness seizures than anxiety or panic attacks
Order EEG to evaluate for epileptiform activity — and when routine EEG is equivocal, pursue prolonged video-EEG monitoring
Order brain MRI, and when initial MRI is equivocal, escalate to 3T with dedicated epilepsy protocol
Obtain basic metabolic panel and CBC to exclude metabolic causes
Initiate antiepileptic drug therapy once diagnosis is established
Counsel patient on driving restrictions and seizure safety
Reassess the prior anxiety diagnosis in light of the new epilepsy diagnosis
contraindicated_actions: Dismissing the episodes as anxiety or panic attacks without adequate neurological workup
Increasing the sertraline dose or adding benzodiazepines as anxiolytics in lieu of proper epilepsy evaluation
Performing lumbar puncture — there is no clinical indication for CSF analysis in this scenario
Prescribing bupropion for anxiety or depression, as it lowers the seizure threshold
Discharging without arranging prolonged video-EEG monitoring when the routine EEG is equivocal and clinical suspicion for epilepsy is significant
Advising the patient that she may continue driving without restriction
key_reasoning_points: The stereotyped nature of the episodes (abrupt onset, brief duration, automatisms, post-ictal confusion) strongly favors focal impaired awareness seizures over anxiety or dissociative episodes, even in a patient with a known anxiety disorder

The vague abdominal discomfort and depersonalization described by the patient are subtle temporal lobe aura equivalents that were misattributed to anxiety — this is a well-documented diagnostic pitfall
An equivocal routine EEG does not exclude epilepsy. Approximately 50% of patients with epilepsy have a normal first EEG. Prolonged monitoring significantly increases diagnostic yield.

Subtle hippocampal sclerosis can be missed on standard 1.5T MRI. A dedicated 3T epilepsy protocol with thin coronal cuts is essential when clinical suspicion persists despite equivocal initial imaging.
The remote history of a febrile seizure in childhood is a risk factor for subsequent mesial temporal sclerosis, supporting the diagnosis even when initial imaging is subtle

Cross-modal concordance (right temporal EEG focus, right hippocampal sclerosis on 3T MRI, right temporal hypometabolism on PET, visuospatial memory deficit on neuropsych) is essential for confident localization
The non-specific white matter foci on MRI are likely incidental and should not distract from the primary temporal lobe pathology — this is a potential red herring at the moderate difficulty level

metadata.estimated_complexity: Moderate — equivocal initial findings requiring escalation of diagnostics, prior misdiagnosis as anxiety
